facts.text: Greece is a southeastern European country made up of a mainland and thousands of islands.
Athens is Greece's capital and one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities in the world.
Ancient Greece played a foundational role in democracy, philosophy, theater, history writing, and mathematics.
The Acropolis and the Parthenon remain among the most famous classical landmarks on Earth.
The Olympic Games began in ancient Greece at Olympia.
Greek mythology — including Zeus, Athena, Apollo, and Hercules — continues to influence books, movies, and language today.
Socrates, Plato, and Aristotle were all Greek thinkers whose work shaped Western philosophy.
Greece has a long coastline and is surrounded by the Aegean, Ionian, and Mediterranean seas.
Santorini and Mykonos are two of Greece's most famous island destinations.
Olive oil, feta, seafood, and grilled meats are staples of Greek cuisine.
Alexander the Great spread Greek language and culture across a huge territory from the Mediterranean to parts of Asia.
Modern Greece hosted the first modern Olympic Games in Athens in 1896.
Greek history bridges ancient city-states, Byzantine heritage, Ottoman rule, and the modern nation-state.
The Greek alphabet influenced later alphabets, including the Latin script used in English.
The Acropolis of Athens is one of the most famous archaeological sites in the world.
Its temples overlook the city and symbolize the legacy of ancient Greece.
Acropolis of Athens is one of the most recognizable places or topics associated with Greece.

Learning about Acropolis of Athens helps explain part of the geography, culture, or history of Greece.
